speaker: interviewer
text: And we're almost-- Make a signal, and he'll quickly the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other thing is I would like to go into my childhood. When you ask the questions [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start, like you said, we'll start with the background, what it was like before the war. And I'll ask you to describe your hometown and your family life and what Jewish life was like. And just tell a story, and we'll progress chronologically and we'll feed into-- I am Carolyn Richmond, the interviewer today. And I'm here with Edith Hass, whose maiden name is Neiderman. And it is September 17, 1995, and are in are Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. And today's interview will proceed in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is September 17, 1995 I'm here with Edith Hass. Neiderman was her maiden name. I'm Carolyn Richmond, and we are in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. And today's interview will proceed in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do state your name and maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Hass. My name is Edith Hass-- maiden name is Neiderman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's November 3, 1926. I'm 69 years old-- I'll be 69.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Satu Mare, Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Abraham Neiderman. My mother's maiden name was Szerena [? Chassa. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had three brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was Goldie, Lillie. My brother was Vernath, Daniel, and Hercu.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Satu Mare. I went to school there until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town of about 55,000 people, and 14,000 were Jewish people living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of home did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a two-family house. We shared it with my uncle. And we were comfortable. My father had a restaurant, and we had a very peaceful and loving family relation with my aunts and uncles. We had a very, very nice family life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were cooked dishes and cold cuts and beverages, drinks, alcohol. And, um, it was a nice comfortable living. We made a nice-- my father made a nice living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home was a three-bedroom apartment. And we lived with my grandfather, who I was very close to. And, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Lepod. And I was very close with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was retired already. He was an older man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a lot of relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: He founded the business, and then he gave it over to my father and my gr-- my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a lot of relatives living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had about 32 uncles, aunts and uncles, and cousins, about 70, I guess, more or less. .

speaker: interviewer
text: Live near you in the same village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same-- some of them lived in the same town. Some of them lived in the Hungary, and some of them lived in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do seem them often?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one who lived out of the country, no. Occasionally, my aunts came to visit my grandfather. We spent some holidays together, and then we visited occasionally, but not very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were holidays like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was beautiful. I felt very privileged and very rich. And I appreciated, then, as a young child, the family life we had. It was very close knit. And even with the aunts and the cousins, we were very, very close. Everyone, each of us, had the same age as a cousin, so we really needed no other friends. We were always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: With holidays in my house-- and they used to come to us because my grandfather-- the aunts and uncles. The center was in our house for getting together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Homemade, [LAUGHS] it was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cook when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never did. I never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school, elementary school. And then I went to a lyceum, which was like high school, and-- but I didn't finish. I only went for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just Jewish students, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was all kind. No, not only Jewish. It was not a private school. It wasn't a parochial school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends-- I had two schoolmates and then the cousins, which I was already close with.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: For fun, we went to a movie or a theater, and we used to walk a lot. We were Shabbat observers, so we walked. There was a nice center of the town. We got together, and we spent-- we had our fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of movies did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had American movies. Shirley Temple was my big fan-- I was big fan of hers. We'd see all those older movies, and there were, uh, nice movies to be seen then. It was not restricted, or you were able to go to see anything because it was different time than this today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your classes like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My classes, as far as large classes, they were, I think, 30, 35.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: We studied in Romanian language. The second language was French, which I studied in high school. But I still remember Romanian. My mother tongue is Hungarian, because my parents, when they were born, and where they went to school was Hungary. And in the house, we spoke Hungarian. But when I started school, I learned the language, Romanian, which I was fluent in, but not practicing it today. It's really hard for me to converse in Romanian, but I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Jewish life like in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very nice. There was ultra-orthodox people, which was the Satmar [INAUDIBLE], which was a very famous Rabbi Teitelbaum. And there were other, smaller synagogues. There was one synagogue my grandfather was the president of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [? Kevrich ?] [? Mishnias, ?] which I don't even know the Hungarian name or the English name of it. He was very respected. He was a very intelligent and very smart man. And they used to come to him for advice, and he was self-taught. And he had lot of knowledge and very intelligent, and he was very respected in the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was education very important in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Education, it was-- they didn't emphasize for the boys, especially, because the religion was very, very important. We were very strict Shabbat observers, and they went to yeshivas. And they learned on their own. They knew how to read and write in Romanian, but they didn't go to higher education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a Hebrew school, or what type of religious training and education did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a private tutor, a lady who tutored how to read in Hebrew the prayers and the meaning of the religion that you studied, but I didn't go to any Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for your summers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summers, well-- we used to have help. We used to have a maid. But in 1940, when the Hungarian occupied the country where we lived, they were not allowed to hire any help. I had to help my mother, because we still had a big household and shopping and cleaning, which was a lot of work to be done. So that's how I spent my time, which I wasn't happy about it then. I was pretty-- sometimes complain, but I had no choice. I had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you spend a lot of time with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we Always had this-- a lot of things in common. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --around when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. That started when the Hungarian occupied the city. There was-- we had to wear the yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, also we felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little by little, they took away the right-- the license-- to sell certain things in stores. Like alcohol beverages my father was not allowed to sell in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been in 1942. 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything in the 1930s that you remember happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930S, under the Romanians? No, there was here and there, but nothing extreme, nothing very uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like they used to call you dirty Jew, here and there. And, um-- or sometimes when they came home from the synagogue, the holiday, they used to throw rocks. But it wasn't, uh, that many occasions that it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngsters, the young, the students, the Romanians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat you any differently in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did things change in the 1940s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in 1944, the German occupied the city. And, uh, then We knew already. We heard stories which they did to other people like Poland and Slovakia, but we didn't know the details, how bad was. They just-- we knew, that it's not going to be good. We saw that black cloud is over us and something very bad is going to be. But we didn't realize it. We couldn't imagine the things that really happened later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about wearing the armbands? When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yellow stars-- that had started by the Hungarians. And, uh, then they went on the street. They made you different remarks. It's a pity that you're Jewish and, you know, what's going to be with you. And they took the men to forced labor, which they took them to the border of Russia. Ukraine was occupied already by the Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- my brother-in-law went, and my cousins went, and we never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did that happened in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was school like during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't go to school anymore then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you stop going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, in '4-- by the time-- when the Hungarians came in, already I didn't go to school. They forced you to, uh, bring, uh-- bring your school books and to write and to not observe your Shabbat. And my father was very much against it, and he didn't want me to continue. And that's how I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all your siblings stop school also then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the boys still went to yeshiva. And elementary schools-- the younger ones still went. The others finished elementary school by that time. And you felt the anti-Semitism, the remarks, what they-- and it was very hard to make a living because you weren't allowed to sell certain things that was rationed, like bread or sugar or flour. So you just had the minimum, really, that you are able to survive on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: The restaurant closed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did it close?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't-- it fell into the ghetto, and no businesses were conducted anymore in that time, really. Our house fell into the ghetto, and two other families moved in with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the ghetto come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. It must have been in March, March or April.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very crowded, and we were very concerned and very, um, very worried what's going to be. We knew that it's not going to be good. Day by day, it was getting worse. We didn't stay in the ghetto long, about two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go to temple even before the ghetto? Did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: The temple we did, but in the ghetto, we didn't because we couldn't. There was no shul in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the shul like right before the ghetto came?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, uh-- they conducted the services. It was-- everybody was worried and everybody was-- you know what the future is going to be, everybody is concerned. But, uh, there was nothing happening in the synagogues then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your temple? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a small. My father was in a small temple. And um, it was, uh-- mostly, I knew everybody, and I had friends there. And I was, in fact, very fortunate that I should be able to go and be there and listen to the prayers, and I enjoyed it. And sitting next to my mother was a treat. And, uh, children didn't stay, in those days, long in temple. They came to a certain prayer, and they went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you where to temple? What type of clothes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like holiday clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi-- there was no special rabbi. It was a very small shul, and the people they conducted the services between themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did things change when they ghetto started to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the ghetto came, it was very sad because you were very limited. We were very crowded, and the food was scarce. And, uh. We were just very worried. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters. My sister came home with her children-- and the younger-- both sisters. And she also-- the Younger one also had a child, and the older one had two children. And uh, two other families with children, so it was really crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other families?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other families were just from the near town. They came from the farms and from the smaller towns, and they concentrated them in the city. So we knew them from before, and we got along. There was no problem to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: --live with them. Food-- there was rations. There was-- every family got a certain amount. We had yet form before. My mother was able to bake her own bread yet because we had the flour, and we had-- but we still had enough to go by.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people get food? Where did it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were places, bakeries, that you had the card, the ration card, where you went and you picked up whatever that was coming to you, and also the sugar and also the whatever else was to be gotten, but very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the days when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I played with my nephews and my nieces. I loved them. And we went outside in the streets, and it was May-- beautiful. The flowers were in bloom, and I was young, and I was-- that is so unfair what's happening. I didn't have the life to live yet, and I don't know what tomorrow is going to be. So when you're young, you don't take things so seriously, you know, as an older person. But I was very concerned what's going to be, and most of the family should be together. We were very afraid we were going to be separated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What had your parents told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They heard a rumor that the youngsters will go to work and the old people and the little ones will stay home. So my mother was worried, where will they go? What will I do as a young girl? What kind of work will they give us? And what supervision is going to be? But it didn't come to that, because after the two weeks, we left. They deported us. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the ghetto for only two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a strong military presence?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, uh-- there was on the gate, by the-- where the-- that was barricaded, the area, the outside. But not in the ghetto, we didn't see that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, first of all, we had to go through a hold. It was like a school. And we had to give your jewelry and whatever you possessed-- your-- the women, their marriage bands. I had earrings and a watch. I had to give it to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you have to give it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were there some soldiers and police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the soldiers, do you remember what kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any Nazis there at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some Germans. They occupied, but they didn't bear the Nazi uniform. They just wore regular German uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you turned in your jewelry and those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, then we went back home. We-- of course we were sad that we had to give in what was-- you know. But it didn't bother us so much, because we were more concerned what else? What's-- what's going to be next? What will they do to us next?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came, and they told us that we'll have to gather the most necessary belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some soldiers. And we have to stay outside, line up front of the house and wait till the turn's going to be that we should-- we'll have to walk to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning of May '44, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What belongings did you gather?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most necessary, like we had some food leftover, a change of clothing, and for the babies, the children's clothing and blankets. And we had to carry it ourselves. So there was a children's carriage, which we piled up on there whatever. And we walked to the station with our neighbors whoever was-- and by this station, the cattle cars were waiting, and they crammed us in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the cattle cars like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, um, that they, um, take animals in, like cattles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no chairs, no nothing, just the floor-- bare floor. And we tried to squeeze in because it was very crowded. They put us 80 people into one cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the family, yes. We were together-- my father, my mother and my brothers. We all were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we were traveling for three days-- three days and three nights--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get out of the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in that cattle car. No, no, and there were small babies. There was a mother with a newborn child. And we had no water all this time. And we was-- my mother used to go to the window was the thing, and beg for water for the babies. But nobody listened, nobody. They wouldn't give us. It was three very long days, and, uh, it was very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there's nobody to talk to. And that's when the Hungarian gave us over to the Germans. Then, they had German patrols. And my mother spoke German. And she begged that they should, you know, that nobody listened. We didn't have any water, and it as very, very difficult. Children was crying. My sister fainted a few times. The air was very stuffy, and it was a very, very hard ex-- very sad experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people talking inside the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were praying. My father was a very strong believer. And then he said, what's going to be? He says, don't worry, God will help us. The help is going to come in. And we questioned him. We said Father, what now? He says, what can I tell you? We still have to hope. We still have to hope. My father had a beard, and we were afraid that they're going to pick on him. So my sister had a scissor and cut his beard. And he had the black beard, yet. And then in the back and front of our eyes, he got white. He became white. He became an old man. And we didn't know what's going to be. We didn't know what's expecting us to happen. We didn't realize. We couldn't imagine. We couldn't imagine. While we were home, before the ghetto, my brother came home one day very said, and he put on his hat. And we couldn't talk to him. And I asked him what happened. He says, I cannot tell you what I heard today. Just please, tell us. Somebody who ran away from Slovakia-- which they occupied, the Germans, before our town, and they took the people away from there-- and he told a horror story about what's happening to people that the Germans take away. We didn't want to believe it. He said this is just a rumor. It can't be. It's impossible that it should be true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told him that there is such place as Auschwitz, and they have an exterminator and how they kill people and how they torture people. And we didn't want to believe it, that it's true, that such a thing exists. After the three days, [BACKGROUND NOISE] we arrived in Auschwitz, very confused and very tired and very exhausted. They rushed us off on the wagon. We weren't able to take anything with us. They said, just leave everything. You're going to get it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were when--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't know. We didn't know. And somebody came over to us which had the striped uniform, that they wore in camp, a man. And he put his lapel-- under his lapel, he had the Magen David-- you know, a Star of David. And he said, trust me, I'm Jewish, and listen to me. I want your sister to give the child to an older person, and I looked much younger than I was. And I want you to straighten yourself up, make yourself taller and older than you are. And you're going to come to somebody that's going to ask you how old you are. Tell that you're older, and make yourself look older. And we took his advice, I didn't know. We thought he's Jewish, he wants the good. He's on our side. And that's what I did. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was from Poland, I think. He spoke Jewish. And um, They separated us. The men lined up separate and the woman, the ladies--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1 separated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had an order-- somebody who spoke to us German-- in German-- soldiers, must've been SS, I guess. And that's what happened. My father with my brothers, they were on one side, and my mother and the rest of the ladies, girls, went on the other side. And then they stayed like five in a row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stood there for awhile, and then the-- they started moving. The line started to move. And my sister didn't know what to do. Should she listen to my-- to that certain person to told her to do what-- to give away the child. And she did, and the older sister said what shall I do? She says, shall I give my children also to an older person? And she was looking around to who she would give it to. But then she decided no, I'm not. Whatever happens to my children, I want to be with them. I'm not going to separate myself. How old were all the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 5 and 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her name was-- the little girl's name was Miriam, and the little boy was named David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any others?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my other sister's was Taube. She had one child who was about four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day like-- it was-- it was-- I don't know the weather. We were just very frustrated, and I didn't even realize what the weather was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you were on line?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were on line-- so always five people went ahead to-- in the line. And when it came to our line. We stopped. And there was Mengele. You heard about him. He was the one who selected people which way to go. And he pointed to my mother and my sister, who was ever in that line-- I had the cousin also who was younger than me-- to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, I know. I didn't know then. I didn't, but I saw picture, and then I know. And he pointed to the left to my mother and my sister, and to me, I was myself, then my-- to the right. And I said, I want to go with my mother. I don't want to-- that's my mother. I spoke a little German. He says, no, you go. And he had a rubber stick in his hand, pointed to my back and give me a push like I should go the other side. And I stopped. I went. There was two doors, like there was a gate. And I look back-- yes, you have to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.
